journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Ultrasonography-based diagnosis of hemorrhage syndrome in adipose tissues in the crest of the neck of heavy horse breeds

contrib-group.aff: 1)Meat Safety Inspection Office in Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto, Japan
2)Department of Large Animal Clinic, Yamaguchi University of Joint Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Yamaguchi, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Sasaki N: nsasaki@yamaguchi-u.ac.jp, Department of Large Animal Clinic, Yamaguchi University of Joint Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Yoshida 1677-1, Yamaguchi-shi, Yamaguchi 753-0841, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2023 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p:
This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0:
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: Hemorrhage syndrome in adipose tissues in the crest of the neck (HSCN), specifically in hemorrhagic adipose tissues on the longitudinally sectioned surface of the neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament, is prevalent in heavy horse breeds. Herein, we aimed to establish an ultrasonographic method to successfully diagnose HSCN in heavy horse breeds and assess its efficacy. Horses with homogeneous echogenicity images were included in the control group, whereas those with linear high-echogenicity images were classified as having HSCN. Horses with confirmed linear high-echogenicity images exhibited pathological features and significantly higher percentages of adipose tissue with hemorrhage than those observed in horses with homogeneous echogenicity images (P<0.01). Our results suggest the effectiveness of ultrasonography in identifying and diagnosing HSCN.

kwd-group.kwd: neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament

body.p: Fat accumulation due to overfeeding is commonly observed in horses [3]. Several heavy horse breeds raised for fattening in Kumamoto Prefecture are reared to have increased body size, which can lead to fat deposition in the adipose tissue at the dorsal nuchal ligament of the neck [4]. Long-term feeding management should be planned to counter this. Hemorrhagic adipose tissues have often been observed in the longitudinal sections of the neck crests of horses during pathological autopsies. This condition is defined as hemorrhage syndrome in adipose tissues in the crest of the neck (HSCN), specifically at the dorsal nuchal ligament. Although there are no characteristic clinical signs of hemorrhagic lesions in horses, economic loss to the farm occurs due to the loss of value of the adipose tissue on the dorsal nuchal ligament of the neck, whose color is preferred white when horses are used for food. Ultrasound imaging is a noninvasive technique used to examine joints and abdominal fat in horses using a portable test equipment [5, 20, 22]. Existing diagnostic and pathological tests are inadequate for the noninvasive examination of the lesion tissues. Therefore, there is a need to develop testing methods that reduce the physical burden on horses. In the present study, we aimed to examine the effectiveness of ultrasonography in identifying HSCN and to establish it as a successful diagnostic method for heavy horse breeds. The observations from this preliminary study are intended to provide a foundation for developing future measures to prevent HSCN.

Twenty-two horses of heavy breeds from a farm in Kumamoto Prefecture (14 females, 8 geldings; mean weight: 979.3 ± 77.1 kg; breed: 11 Belgian mixed, 4 Breton, 4 Percheron, and 3 other varieties) were used as test horses. The test horses were fattening and healthy with no abnormal findings other than locomotor diseases (excessively long hooves) and were raised in stalls (10–20 horses per pen). Horses were fed 4 kg of roughage diet (Italian ryegrass, rice straw, and rice whole crop silage) and 16 kg of concentrated diet (compound feed, soybean meal, and brewer’s grain) twice daily. In addition, water was freely available. HSCN occurs in horses weighing 1,012 ± 105.1 kg on average [4]. The horses were classified based on hemorrhagic adipose tissues on the longitudinal cut surface of the neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament and previous examinations of other horses [4] as follows: control group−unconfirmed hemorrhagic adipose tissue, HSCN group−hemorrhagic adipose tissue. A total of 10 horses (mean weight: 958.5 ± 81.5 kg; 7 females, 3 geldings) were classified as the control group and 12 horses (mean weight: 996.7 ± 68.6 kg; 7 females, 5 geldings) as the HSCN group. Before the autopsy, neck circumference (NC) was measured at the front 25% of the neck, stretching from the back of the head to the withers (NC25%), the middle 50% (NC50%), and the caudal 75% (NC75%) (Fig. 1Fig. 1.
Monitoring of neck circumference (NC) measurement sites using ultrasound. The solid and dotted lines indicate the sites where the NC25%, NC50%, and NC75% values were measured (left). The position indicated in the image by the ultrasonic probe above the NC50% line denotes the area at which the diagnostic 5.0 MHz convex ultrasound transducer should be applied (right).). Since the preferred HSCN site was at the NC50%, the neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament was evaluated using an ultrasound system (MyLabOne VET; Esaote Europe B.V., Maastricht, Holland) with a 5.0-MHz convex probe in B-mode from the lateral position to the NC50% (Fig. 1).

This study was approved by an institution equivalent to an ethics review committee from the Meat Safety Inspection Office in Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto, and the Animal Care and Use Committee at Yamaguchi University, Japan.

Statistical analysis was performed using Statcel-The Useful Add-in Forms on Excel fourth ed. (OMS Publishing Inc., Saitama, Japan), JMP Pro 14.00 (SAS Institute, Cary, NC, USA), and EZR (Saitama Medical Center, Jichi Medical University, Saitama, Japan). Mann–Whitney U test was used to determine the differences between the average NC values of the control and HSCN groups. Fisher’s exact test was used to determine the association between the features of ultrasound echogenicity images and pathological status of adipose tissues with hemorrhage. A P-value of <5% (P<0.05) was considered significant.

Autopsies of horses in the control group revealed that the adipose tissues in the longitudinal sections of the neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament were white to yellowish-white, slightly hard, and elastic, with a rough dorsal surface (Fig. 2aFig. 2.
Histological and ultrasonography images of the nuchal crest adipose tissue. a. Histological image of normal adipose tissue. b. Ultrasonography image of normal adipose tissue. c. Histological image of hemorrhage in HSCN (red circle). d. Ultrasonography image of hemorrhage in HSCN (red circle). White linear high-echogenic images (white arrow) and black low-echogenic images (black arrow). Scale: 10 cm (a, c), 5 cm (b, d). HSCN: hemorrhage syndrome in adipose tissues in the crest of the neck.). Concordantly, ultrasound examination indicated homogeneous echogenicity in the images (Fig. 2b). In contrast, an autopsy of horses with HSCN revealed adipose tissues that were reddish brown to dark red, semi-dome-shaped, and elastic, centered around the border of the nuchal ligament. The hemorrhage did not penetrate the subcutaneous tissue surface of the neck crest (Fig. 2c). Ultrasound examination revealed white linear high-echogenic images at a 5–10 cm depth and black low-echogenic images between the white linear high-echogenic images (Fig. 2d).

Test horses were classified into four groups to determine the association between ultrasound echogenicity in the images and pathological status of adipose tissues with hemorrhage. The first group comprised 11 horses with linear high-echogenic images. The second group included one horse with a homogeneous echogenicity image. Hemorrhagic adipose tissue was observed in the neck crest in both the groups. The third group included two horses with linear high-echogenicity images and the fourth group comprised eight horses with homogeneous echogenicity images. The horses from the third and fourth groups had adipose tissues with no hemorrhage. Our results suggested that horses with linear high-echogenicity images exhibit pathological and significantly higher percentages of adipose tissue with hemorrhage than those observed in horses with homogeneous echogenicity images (P<0.01). The sensitivity and specificity of ultrasonography were 0.917 [95% confidence interval (CI): 0.615–0.998] and 0.800 (95% CI: 0.444–0.975), respectively. We compared the average NC between the control and HSCN groups. The average NC25% value in the HSCN group (121.8 ± 7.6 cm) was significantly higher than that in the control group (113.3 ± 6.1 cm) (P<0.05) (Table 1Table 1.Results of physical examination and the average neck circumference (NC) 25%, NC50%, and NC75% values
FeatureControlHSCN
Weight (kg)958.5 ± 81.5996.7 ± 68.5
Age (year)2.5 ± 0.502.7 ± 0.47
NC25% value (cm)113.3 ± 6.1121.8 ± 7.6*
NC50% value (cm)143.2 ± 5.8150.5 ± 6.1*
NC75% value (cm)161.4 ± 6.9170.4 ± 5.9*
*P<0.05. The average NC25% value in the HSCN group (121.8 ± 7.6 cm) was significantly higher than that in the control group (113.3 ± 6.1 cm). The average NC50% value in the HSCN group (150.5 ± 6.1 cm) was significantly higher than that in the control group (143.2 ± 5.8 cm). The average NC75% value in the HSCN group (170.4 ± 5.9 cm) was significantly higher than that in the control group (161.4 ± 6.9 cm). HSCN: hemorrhage syndrome in adipose tissues in the crest of the neck.). The average NC50% value in the HSCN group (150.5 ± 6.1 cm) was significantly higher than that in the control group (143.2 ± 5.8 cm) (P<0.05) (Table 1). The average NC75% value in the HSCN group (170.4 ± 5.9 cm) was significantly higher than that in the control group (161.4 ± 6.9 cm) (P<0.05) (Table 1).

We used a portable test equipment to examine the effectiveness of ultrasonography in identifying HSCN in heavy horse breeds. The presence of hemorrhages in the adipose tissue on the longitudinally cut surface of neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament was assessed in heavy-breed horses. Ultrasound examination revealed linear high-echogenicity images for hemorrhagic adipose tissues. Horses with linear high-echogenicity images had significantly higher percentages of hemorrhagic adipose tissue than those observed in horses with homogeneous echogenicity images. Ultrasonography had high sensitivity and specificity. Adipose tissues on longitudinal sections of neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament of heavy horse breeds are typically suspected of having lipomatosis based on histological analysis [4]. Perirenal lipomatosis in cows and lipoma in humans and dogs presents linear high-echogenicity on ultrasonography images compared to the homogeneous echogenicity images of the adjacent normal adipose tissues [10, 19, 21]. Histopathological examination remains the gold standard in humans and horses; nonetheless, the diagnostic capability of ultrasound examination is equal to or superior to that of histopathological analysis in that a diagnosis can be made promptly on the spot without waiting for the pathology results. [13, 23]. In Thoroughbred racehorses with osteochondrosis, the sensitivity and specificity of ultrasonography were reportedly 0.92 and 0.97, respectively [14]. The X-ray absorption rates of adipose and adjacent tissues are similar; therefore, obtaining contrast in X-ray images is challenging. Conversely, the difference in acoustic impedance at the interface of the adipose and adjacent tissues can be captured using ultrasound imaging; hence, ultrasound examination can aid in the better visualization of adipose tissues [7]. Moreover, ultrasound aids in accurately depicting lesions [16]. These findings suggest that ultrasound examination may facilitate an accurate depiction of adipose tissues on the longitudinal sections of neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament and HSCN lesions. It has been reported that adipose tissue in the neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament, where hemorrhage is observed, histopathological analysis showed only collagen fibers, which are connective tissues [4]. We speculate that tissues at bleeding sites have a higher acoustic impedance than that in non-hemorrhagic adipose tissues. Even without clear hemorrhagic lesions as the initial step leading to hemorrhage in the HSCN, it may be possible to detect signs of HSCN by ultrasonographic examination and demonstration of linear high-echogenicity images of neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament.

The NC25% value of horses with fat accumulation in the dorsal nuchal ligament is significantly higher than that of horses without fat deposition [9]. Subsequently, the HSCN group had significantly higher NC25% value than that of the control group. Measuring the NC values is effective for diagnosing obesity in humans [1, 8, 18], and higher NC values are associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular diseases [15]. The hemorrhage observed in the adipose tissues of heavy horse breeds is likely attributable to the rupture of blood vessels in adipose tissues [4]. Previous studies have reported significant correlations between NC and fat thickness values in the dorsal nuchal ligament [2, 6] and between NC25% values and the degree of fat in horses [12, 17]. High fat accumulation in the neck at the dorsal nuchal ligament is reportedly associated with high NC25% values in horses [9, 11]. These findings suggest that the NC25% values in heavy horse breeds can aid the diagnosis of HSCN.

To our knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate the effectiveness of ultrasound imaging in diagnosing HSCN in heavy horse breeds. However, the study had several limitations. The sample size was small, and the horses were raised in specific areas of Kumamoto Prefecture. Therefore, generalizing these results to other heavy horse breeds requires further validation. Further research is warranted with a higher number of test horses from diverse regions.

In conclusion, evaluation of neck fat at the dorsal nuchal ligament from the lateral NC50% side using ultrasonography may facilitate the accurate representation of adipose tissues and HSCN lesions in heavy horse breeds. These results suggest that ultrasonography may aid in the effective identification and diagnosis of HSCN and can serve as the basis for future investigations that aim to evaluate non-clinical symptoms of HSCN. Ultrasonography of the HSCN in heavy horse breeds has not been previously validated. Therefore, the results of this study may contribute significantly to the early detection and development of measures to prevent the occurrence of HSCN.

sec.title: CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest associated with this manuscript.
